The **1900 United States census**, conducted by the [Census Office](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") on June 1, 1900,[[1]](#cite_note-1) determined the resident [population](/wiki/Population "Population") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") to be 76,212,168, an increase of 21.01% from the 62,979,766 persons [enumerated](/wiki/Enumeration "Enumeration") during the [1890 census](/wiki/1890_United_States_census "1890 United States census"). It was the last census to be conducted before the founding of the permanent United States Census Bureau.

The census saw the nation's largest city, [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), more than double in size due to the [consolidation with Brooklyn](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York"), becoming in the process the first U.S. city to record a population growth of over three million — [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") was previously the 4th largest city in the United States on its own.

Oversight of the statistics was by Frederick Howard Wines and [Walter F. Willcox](/wiki/Walter_Francis_Willcox "Walter Francis Willcox").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Census questions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1900_United_States_census&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Census questions")]

The 1900 census collected the following information:[[3]](#cite_note-3)

* address
* name
* relationship to head of family
* sex
* race (listed as "Color or race" on the census)
* age, month and year born
* marital status and, if married, number of years married
* for women, number of children born and number now living
* place of birth of person, and their parents
* if foreign born, year of immigration and whether naturalized
* occupation
* months not employed
* school
* ability to speak English
* whether on a farm
* home owned or rented, and, if owned, whether mortgaged

Full documentation for the 1900 census, including census forms and enumerator instructions, is available from the [Integrated Public Use Microdata Series](/wiki/Integrated_Public_Use_Microdata_Series "Integrated Public Use Microdata Series").

Statistician [Frederick Ludwig Hoffman](/wiki/Frederick_Ludwig_Hoffman "Frederick Ludwig Hoffman") criticized the conduct of the census.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Data availability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1900_United_States_census&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Data availability")]

The original census enumeration sheets were [microfilmed](/wiki/Microform "Microform") by the Census Bureau in the 1940s, after which the original sheets were destroyed.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The microfilmed census is available in rolls from the [National Archives and Records Administration](/wiki/National_Archives_and_Records_Administration "National Archives and Records Administration"). Several organizations also host images of the microfilmed census online, and digital indices.

[Microdata](/wiki/Microdata_(statistics) "Microdata (statistics)") from the 1900 census are freely available through [IPUMS](/wiki/Integrated_Public_Use_Microdata_Series "Integrated Public Use Microdata Series"). [Aggregate data](/wiki/Aggregate_data "Aggregate data") for small areas, together with electronic boundary files, can be downloaded from the [National Historical Geographic Information System](/wiki/National_Historical_Geographic_Information_System "National Historical Geographic Information System").
